title: Today's AI Brief: Apple Approves Poke as the First AI Agent on Messages for Business
summary: Apple approves Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform, and OpenAI outlines its public policy agenda for AI.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Apple approves Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform
imageCaption: Apple approves Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform
imageSourceTitle: Apple approves Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform

body.text: I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of AI. First up, Apple has approved Poke as the first AI agent on its Messages for Business platform. For those who might not know, Poke is a startup that's making it simple to use AI agents by basically turning them into text message conversations. This is a big deal because it shows how AI is becoming more accessible and user-friendly, even for businesses.
Poke on Apple's Messages for Business

So, what does this mean? Well, previously, Apple's Messages for Business platform was designed more for human-to-human interactions. Now, with Poke on board, businesses can use AI to interact with customers in a more automated way. It's like having a super-smart chatbot that can help with customer inquiries, bookings, or even just answering frequently asked questions. My read is that this could significantly change how businesses handle customer service.

Also on the board, OpenAI has outlined its public policy agenda for AI. They're focusing on safety, protecting youth, workforce transition, and establishing global standards to ensure AI benefits society. It's a pretty comprehensive approach, acknowledging that AI isn't just about tech - it's about people and society too. What stands out to me is their emphasis on safety and youth protection, which are critical as AI becomes more pervasive.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
